learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate an understanding of teaching as a dynamic and challenging form of professional work.

Demonstrate an understanding of the diverse backgrounds, development and characteristics of students, how these may affect learning, and teaching strategies to meet the specific learning needs of students across the full range of abilities. (APST 1.1, 1.5)
Know and understand literacy and numeracy teaching strategies and their application in learning areas. (APST 2.5)

Identify and implement teaching strategies, including the use of ICTs, that provide students with appropriate, challenging learning goals and an engaging yet safe and supportive learning environment. (APST 2.6, 3.1)

Demonstrate understanding of assessment strategies (including informal and formal, diagnostic, formative and summative approaches), the purpose of assessment moderation to support consistent and comparable judgements and a range of strategies for keeping accurate and reliable records for reporting to students and parents/carers. (APST 5.1, 5.3, 5.5)
Demonstrate broad knowledge of strategies that can be used to evaluate teaching programs to improve student learning. (APST 3.6)
Recognise and understand the place of legislative, school and employing authority requirements and assessment data in the teaching and learning process. (APST 1.6, 5.4, 7.2)

Demonstrate knowledge of relevant codes of practices and critical reflection on the technical and communication skills required to interact effectively with students, care givers and fellow professionals in an educational environment. (APST 7.1)
Demonstrate clear, fluent, and coherent communication skills consistent with personal, professional and academic conventions.
Demonstrate all teaching and professional requirements of the professional experience placement. (APST 1.1, 2.1, 2.2, 2.5, 4.2, 4.3, 6.1, 6.3)

assessments.task_description:
Students must read the Policies and Procedures for Professional Experience Placements document and respond to the statement declaration on Blackboard by the due date. Failure to do so will result in a withdrawal of the placement and the awarding of a grade of Fail for the course. 

APST: 7.1, 7.2

Complete your agreement to the following statement in Blackboard: 

I have read, understood and will abide by, the Policies and Procedures for Professional Experience Placements.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Completion of declaration statement on Blackboard to acknowledge that the student has read, understood and will abide by the policies and procedures.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission of this assessment will result in a failing grade
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Students are required to successfully complete a 15 day block of a supervised and assessed professional experience in an approved school setting. The placement will be arranged only by the School of Education.

  • The first 2 to 3 days to be observation and settling into school environment and context, as well as planning and consulting with supervising teacher(s) for future teaching
  • Towards end of Week 1 to start teaching segments of lessons (e.g. 10 mins or 15 mins, half a lesson, or even a full lesson – this might be the start of the lesson, the conclusion, a lesson quiz, discussion, group activity, reading groups, explaining a concept, etc).
  • Full lessons to commence half way through the placement and to continue until end of placement.


During the practicum, students are expected to demonstrate competence across the APSTs. To do this, students will demonstrate that they have undertaken the inherent requirements of the role of a teacher. This means that students at the very least, will be expected to:

  • attend the school at least half an hour before and after school start and finish times to discuss that day's and subsequent days' expectations
  • attend any meetings held during the time of the practicum, e.g. departmental or whole of staff meetings or any professional development meetings where attendance is approved by the school, participate in extracurricular activities etc available during the block.


Students are expected to have read, and abide by the School of Education Policies & Procedures document for Professional Experience Placements.

APST: 2.1, 2.2, 2.5, 2.6, 3.1, 4.2, 4.3, 6.3, 7.1, 7.2
assessments.submission_guidelines: Upload Final Evaluation report via the Blackboard link in the Assessment folder
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission of assessment without approved extension will result in a failing grade.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
From your 15-day professional experience placement you are required to collate and present a portfolio containing a summary of observations, planning notes and a personal teaching philosophy.


Part A – Summary of observations

You are to prepare a 2000-word report that summarises your observations under the following five headings (the guiding questions are illustrative of areas to consider):

1.

Understanding the school environment and being a teacher;

What is it like to be a teacher in this school? What kinds of duties and responsibilities did you observe that were new to you? What did you learn about how teachers communicate with students, colleagues, parents and other stakeholders? What roles did school policy, procedures and/or legislative requirements play in what you observed? How do you see yourself taking on what is expected of teachers?

2.

Knowing your learners (physical, social and intellectual development and characteristics of students and how these may affect learning);

What did you notice about how individual students learn and develop and what affects their learning and development? What did you observe about the organisation of learning (teaching strategies/activities, structures – the what, why and how) to promote student development? What did you learn about differentiation and how to cater for individual students’ needs? What are some extra-curricular activities that you noticed and what opportunities did these create for student development? How were ICTs used in the school and/or classroom to support students?

3.

Strategies to support full participation of students with disability;

What did you observe about how students with disability were supported, including ICTs? Did you notice policies, procedures or legislative requirements in relation to students with disability?

4.

Practical approaches to manage challenging behaviour;

What practical strategies did you observe for classroom management? How were students and teachers supported in relation to challenging behaviour, including policies or procedures?

5.

Assessment (formative, summative, diagnostic, systemic data; moderation of assessment and reporting mechanisms, the purpose of keeping accurate and reliable records).

What kinds of assessment practices (formative, summative, diagnostic) did you observe? What did you notice about how teachers keep accurate and reliable records and report on student achievement? What did you learn about moderation to ensure comparable judgements?


Part B - Planning notes and reflections

During the placement you have had the opportunity to plan for and present segments of lessons/mini lessons and possibly full lessons.

  1. You are to include three examples of plans that you created and presented, and provide a personal reflection (150-200 words) for each. Your reflection should address what went well and what you would improve for next time.
  2. Based on these personal reflections and feedback provided by your supervising teacher(s), you are required to identify three professional learning goals that will guide your next professional experience placement. You should link your professional learning goals to relevant Australian Professional Standards for Teachers and their focus areas (you may present your goals as dot points).


Part C - Personal teaching philosophy

In reference to Part A and B and through reflection on the professional experience placement and your journey to date in the program, prepare a 500-word personal teaching philosophy that captures and presents how you will aim to ensure that your teaching has a positive impact on student learning. This statement should also identify your values, beliefs and ideas about teaching and the type of teacher you will aim to become. 

The three domains from the Australian Professional Standards for Teachers may help you frame your philosophy.

This will be an important and valuable statement to revisit as you progress through the professional experience courses and your program.


APST: 1.1, 1.6, 2.1, 2.2, 4.3, 5.3, 5.5, 6.1, 6.3
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission of assessment without approved extension request via myUQ system will result in a failing grade.

assessments.task_description:
You are required to investigate and critically analyse the ways in which literacy and numeracy initiatives are implemented in your school and specifically in the classrooms you have been observing.

Prepare a report of 2000 words that clearly and coherently demonstrates your knowledge and understanding of recent developments in literacy and numeracy and how that is carried out in your specific school’s context. You must include a context for the report and a literacy and numeracy profile of the students in your placement school that draws on systemic assessment data for the school, class and focus students (school analysis e.g., PATM, PATR, NAPLAN). Your report should also evaluate a specific literacy or numeracy teaching strategy or program at your placement school and its purported relationship with student learning (e.g., interpretation of a specific activity, assessment or student artefact). Your report should conclude with “I wonder” statements about ways to adapt/modify/change the strategy/program for the purpose of improving student learning.

Your report will be supported by evidence from your placement school with examples or case studies drawn from the classrooms you have been observing. You will also support your writing with references to academic literature.

Your report should also demonstrate your knowledge and understanding of the following focus areas:

o  General literacy and numeracy teaching strategies and their application in teaching areas

o  Strategies for differentiating teaching to meet the specific learning needs of students across the full range of abilities (specifically in literacy and numeracy)

o  Assessment strategies, including informal and formal, diagnostic, formative and/or summative approaches to assess student learning (specifically in literacy and numeracy).

Please make sure you de-identify the school, teachers and students in your report.


APST: 1.5, 2.5, 3.6, 5.1, 5.4

learning_activities.topic:
Entering the profession: course, assessment and placement expectations

• Introduce EDUC2740 as the Year 2 primary professional experience course.

• Overview AT1, AT2, AT3 and AT4, including pass/fail requirements and placement connection.

• Explain 15-day placement structure: observation, assisted teaching, lesson segments and progress toward a full lesson.

• Introduce professional expectations, Blue Card, placement communication and early questions.


APST: 6.1, 6.3, 7.1, 7.2


Learning outcomes: L01, L08, L09, L10
learning_activities.topic_title: Entering the profession: course, assessment and placement expectations

APST: 6.1, 6.3, 7.1, 7.2

learning_activities.topic:
Being a teacher: school life, professional work and observation skills

• Explore teaching as dynamic professional work, including: duties, relationships, routines, policies and responsibilities beyond classroom instruction.

• Introduce professional reflection as a process of noticing, interpreting, learning from feedback and identifying next steps.

• Develop practical observation skills: what to notice, how to take useful notes and how to separate description from interpretation.

• Practice observing examples of teacher communication with students, colleagues and families.


APST: 5.5, 6.3, 7.1, 7.2


Learning outcomes: L01, L07, L08, L09, L10
learning_activities.topic_title: Being a teacher: school life, professional work and observation skills
learning_activities.topic_description: Being a teacher: school life, professional work and observation skills • Explore teaching as dynamic professional work, including: duties, relationships, routines, policies and responsibilities beyond classroom instruction. • Introduce professional reflection as a process of noticing, interpreting, learning from feedback and identifying next steps. • Develop practical observation skills: what to notice, how to take useful notes and how to separate description from interpretation. • Practice observing examples of teacher communication with students, colleagues and families. APST: 5.5, 6.3, 7.1, 7.2

learning_activities.topic:
Knowing your learners: development, characteristics and implications for teaching

• Examine physical, social and intellectual development in primary learners.

• Identify how learner characteristics, background knowledge, confidence, language, peer relationships and developmental needs may affect learning.

• Practice using classroom scenarios to infer reasonable implications for teaching.


APST: 1.1, 1.5, 3.1


Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L09, L10
learning_activities.topic_title: Knowing your learners: development, characteristics and implications for teaching
learning_activities.topic_description: Knowing your learners: development, characteristics and implications for teaching • Examine physical, social and intellectual development in primary learners. • Identify how learner characteristics, background knowledge, confidence, language, peer relationships and developmental needs may affect learning. • Practice using classroom scenarios to infer reasonable implications for teaching. APST: 1.1, 1.5, 3.1

learning_activities.topic:
Differentiation and inclusive learning across the full range of abilities

Differentiation:

• Introduce differentiation of content, process, product, support, grouping and learning environment.

• Apply differentiation to primary lesson segments with achievable challenge for varied learners.

• Analyse how literacy and numeracy needs can be supported through scaffolds, modelling and targeted practice.

Students with a disability:

• Introduce practical strategies that support participation and learning for students with disability or learning difference.

• Examine reasonable adjustments, assistive technologies and inclusive classroom routines.

• Discuss legislative, school and employing authority requirements in relation to disability inclusion.


APST: 1.6, 1.5, 2.6, 4.1, 7.2


Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L05, L06, L07, L09
learning_activities.topic_title: Differentiation and inclusive learning across the full range of abilities

Students with a disability: • Introduce practical strategies that support participation and learning for students with disability or learning difference. • Examine reasonable adjustments, assistive technologies and inclusive classroom routines. • Discuss legislative, school and employing authority requirements in relation to disability inclusion. APST: 1.6, 1.5, 2.6, 4.1, 7.2

learning_activities.topic:
Planning for learning: goals, curriculum links and lesson sequencing

• Practice using the curriculum to write clear learning goals and success criteria.

• Map curriculum content into logical teaching and learning sequences.

• Design a short lesson segment with scaffolding, resources and an achievable challenge.


APST: 2.1, 2.2, 3.1, 6.3


Learning outcomes: L04, L06, L09, L10

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching strategies: modelling, questioning, scaffolding and clear instruction

• Explore practical teaching strategies such as: explicit teaching, modelling, guided practice, questioning, discussion and consolidation.

• Practice giving clear instructions for short classroom activities.

• Micro-plan a 10–15 minute lesson segment appropriate for early placement teaching.

• Use peer feedback to refine explanation, sequencing and student engagement.


APST: 2.1, 2.2, 3.1, 4.2, 6.3


Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L06, L09, L10

learning_activities.topic:
Classroom management 1: routines, organisation and clear instructions

• Examine how classroom layout, transitions, routines, materials and grouping shape participation and behaviour.

• Practice giving concise, sequential, age-appropriate instructions.

• Identify proactive strategies that reduce confusion, waiting time and disengagement.


APST: 4.2, 1.1, 3.1, 6.3


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L08, L10

learning_activities.topic:
Classroom management 2: responding to challenging behaviour

• Identify practical approaches to manage low-level, escalating and persistent behaviour concerns.

• Compare preventative, corrective, restorative and relational approaches.

• Practice responding to behaviour scenarios using calm, specific and proportionate teacher language.


APST: 4.3, 4.2, 7.1, 6.3


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L08, L10

learning_activities.topic:
Literacy strategies across the primary curriculum

• Identify literacy demands across primary learning areas, including: vocabulary, oral language, reading, comprehension and writing.

• Analyse strategies such as: vocabulary routines, think-alouds, sentence stems, modelled reading/writing and graphic organisers.

• Connect literacy strategies to: differentiation, assessment evidence and student learning.


APST: 2.5, 1.5, 5.1, 3.6


Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L05, L06, L09

APST: 2.5, 1.5, 5.1, 3.6

learning_activities.topic:
Numeracy strategies and ICTs across the primary curriculum

• Identify numeracy demands across the curriculum, including: data, measurement, time, spatial reasoning, patterns and quantitative reasoning.

• Explore numeracy strategies such as: concrete-representational-abstract progression, visual models, number talks, worked examples and data interpretation.

• Evaluate purposeful ICT use that expands curriculum learning, accessibility, collaboration or feedback.


APST: 2.5, 2.6, 1.5, 3.1, 5.1


Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L05, L09, L10

learning_activities.topic:
Assessment, data, moderation, records and reporting

• Compare informal, formal, diagnostic, formative and summative assessment in primary classrooms.

• Interpret examples of school, class and student data, such as NAPLAN, PAT-R, PAT-M or classroom assessment evidence.

• Explain moderation, consistent judgement, reliable records and reporting to students and parents/carers.


APST: 5.1, 5.3, 5.4, 5.5, 3.6


Learning outcomes: L05, L06, L07, L09

learning_activities.topic:
Placement readiness, assessment consolidation and professional obligations

• Outline ethics, safety, duty of care, confidentiality, child protection, statutory obligations and professional boundaries.

• Revisit AT2 Final Evaluation report expectations, AT3 portfolio structure and AT4 literacy/numeracy report requirements.

• Revisit what evidence students should collect during placement, including: observations, planning notes, reflections, feedback, assessment/data notes and de-identified examples.

• Revisit placement communication, feedback-seeking, professionalism and how to respond appropriately to supervising teacher guidance.

• Revisit how supervising teacher feedback can inform professional learning goals and future practice.


APST: 5.5, 6.1, 6.3, 7.1, 7.2


Learning outcomes: L01, L07, L08, L09, L10
